bibliographic_data.title: X THEN X: Manipulation of Same-System Runoff Elections
bibliographic_data.abstract: Do runoffs, using the same voting rule as the initial election but just on the winning candidates, increase or decrease the complexity of manipulation? Does allowing revoting in the runoff increase or decrease the complexity relative to just having a runoff without revoting? For both weighted and unweighted voting, we show that even for election systems with simple winner problems the complexity of manipulation, manipulation with runoffs, and manipulation with revoting runoffs are independent, in the abstract. On the other hand, for some important, well-known election systems we determine what holds for each of these cases. For no such systems do we find runoffs lowering complexity, and for some we find that runoffs raise complexity. Ours is the first paper to show that for natural, unweighted election systems, runoffs can increase the manipulation complexity.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper shows that for some important, well-known election systems, same-system runoff elections can either raise or lower the complexity of manipulation, and provides the first example where runoffs increase manipulation complexity for natural, unweighted election systems.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors analyze the computational complexity of manipulation in various two-stage voting scenarios, including runoffs and revoting runoffs, using different weighted and unweighted voting rules. They demonstrate that the complexity can be independent or dependent on the initial system's complexity.
outcomes: For some election systems, same-system runoffs increase the complexity of manipulation.
Same-system runoffs do not lower the complexity of manipulation for any natural, unweighted election systems.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper explores how runoff elections, using the same voting rule as the initial election but just on the winning candidates, affect the complexity of manipulation. The authors show that even for election systems with simple winner problems, the complexity of manipulation, manipulation with runoffs, and manipulation with revoting runoffs are independent in the abstract. However, they also determine what holds for specific important, well-known election systems. For some such systems, same-system runoffs can increase the complexity of manipulation. The paper provides the first example where natural, unweighted election systems see an increase in manipulation complexity due to runoffs.
